topic: Curated factual technical knowledge compiled by the community

entries.q: What is the purpose of the Book of Secret Knowledge repository?
entries.a: The Book of Secret Knowledge repository is a collection of various materials and tools used daily in work, containing useful information gathered in one piece, aimed towards System and Network administrators, DevOps, Pentesters, and Security Researchers.

entries.q: What type of license does the Book of Secret Knowledge repository use?
entries.a: The Book of Secret Knowledge repository uses the MIT License.

entries.q: How can contributors participate in the Book of Secret Knowledge repository?
entries.a: Contributors can participate by making pull requests, adding valid and well-reasoned explanations about their changes or comments, and following the contributing guidelines.

entries.q: What is the target audience for the Book of Secret Knowledge repository?
entries.a: The target audience for the Book of Secret Knowledge repository is System and Network administrators, DevOps, Pentesters, and Security Researchers, but it is open to everyone.

entries.q: What is the RSS/Atom feed used for in the Book of Secret Knowledge repository?
entries.a: The RSS/Atom feed is used to keep informed about all changes and updates to the repository.

entries.q: What are some of the categories included in the Book of Secret Knowledge repository?
entries.a: The categories included in the repository are CLI Tools, GUI Tools, Web Tools, Systems/Services, Networks, Containers/Orchestration, Manuals/Howtos/Tutorials, Inspiring Lists, Blogs/Podcasts/Videos, Hacking/Penetration Testing, and more.

entries.q: What is the purpose of the Table of Contents in the Book of Secret Knowledge repository?
entries.a: The Table of Contents is used to organize and navigate the various categories and topics included in the repository.

entries.q: What are some examples of CLI Tools included in the Book of Secret Knowledge repository?
entries.a: Examples of CLI Tools included in the repository are GNU Bash, Zsh, tclsh, bash-it, Oh My ZSH!, and more.
